title: Level 15: System Stability (Watchdog Timer)
aim: To use the Watchdog Timer (WDT) to automatically reset a crashed or deadlocked microcontroller.
objective: Understand how the separate on-chip Watchdog oscillator functions, configure WDTCSR for various timeout lengths, and execute the wdt_reset() macro.
theory:
What is a Watchdog Timer?

In the real world, software can crash. A robot driving a car will crash if an infinite loop holds up the main processing. A Watchdog Timer (WDT) is a completely separate hardware timer (running on its own internal 128kHz oscillator) whose sole purpose is to count up. If it ever reaches its specified timeout value, it violently forces a hard reset on the entire ATmega328P.

Feeding the Dog

The only way to prevent a reset is for the main software to routinely 'feed' or 'kick' the dog by sending it a reset signal. In C, we do this by calling the wdt_reset() function inside our main loop. If our main loop freezes unexpectedly, wdt_reset() is never called. The watchdog keeps ticking, eventually hitting its timeout and generating a System Reset.

Watchdog Reset Flag (WDRF)

When the system restarts, you might want to know WHY it restarted. The MCUSR (MCU Status Register) contains a WDRF flag. If WDRF is 1 immediately after booting, you know a watchdog reset occurred!

pretest.question: What is the consequence of entering an infinite `while(1)` loop that does NOT contain `wdt_reset()` when the Watchdog Timer is enabled?
options: The Watchdog Timer resets the microcontroller completely when the timeout period elapses
The CPU runs at double the clock speed
pretest.explanation: The Watchdog cannot be stopped by software bugs. If it is not 'fed', it will inevitably force a hardware reset.

pretest.question: What is the primary purpose of the DDRx register in the ATmega328P?
options: To configure pins as input or output
pretest.explanation: DDR configures the direction of each GPIO pin.

pretest.question: Which header file provides basic I/O register definitions for AVR?
pretest.explanation: <avr/io.h> provides register definitions for all AVR peripherals.

pretest.question: What does the volatile keyword tell the C compiler?
options: May change unexpectedly, so do not optimize reads
pretest.explanation: volatile prevents the compiler from caching the variable.

pretest.question: How many GPIO pins does the ATmega328P have in total?
pretest.explanation: 23 GPIO pins across Port B, C, and D.

pretest.question: What is the maximum clock frequency of the ATmega328P?
pretest.explanation: Supports up to 20MHz, though Arduino UNO uses 16MHz.

pretest.question: What is the purpose of sei()?
pretest.explanation: sei() sets the global interrupt enable flag in SREG.

pretest.question: Which AVR memory type retains data after power-off?
What is the width of each ATmega328P port register?
pretest.explanation: The ATmega328P is an 8-bit MCU; all port registers are 8 bits wide.

pretest.question: What is bit manipulation?
options: Operating on individual bits using bitwise operators
pretest.explanation: Bit manipulation uses AND, OR, XOR, and SHIFT.

procedure: Include <avr/wdt.h> at the top of the program.
Wire an LED to Pin 13 to serve as an indicator.
In setup, blink the LED rapidly 5 times to prove the ATmega328P has booted up.
Enable the Watchdog Timer with a 2-second timeout using wdt_enable(WDTO_2S);.
In the main infinite loop, turn the LED ON.
Now intentionally freeze the program with an empty infinite loop: while(1);.
Observe the LED. 2 seconds after the empty loop begins, the chip will forcefully reset, and you will see the 5 rapid boot-up blinks again.

posttest.question: Where does the Watchdog Timer get its clock signal?
options: From its own dedicated on-chip 128kHz oscillator
posttest.explanation: The Watchdog relies on an independent internal RC oscillator so that it continues to function even if the main CPU crystal dramatically fails.

posttest.question: Why is it good practice to use #define for pin numbers?
posttest.explanation: #define creates named constants for self-documenting code.

posttest.question: What tool converts C source to .hex for AVR?
posttest.explanation: avr-gcc compiles C to AVR machine code.

posttest.question: What is the difference between PORTB and PINB?
options: PORTB writes output; PINB reads actual pin voltages
posttest.explanation: PORTB drives outputs; PINB reads real-time pin states.

posttest.question: What happens to SRAM when power is removed?
What is the stack used for in AVR?
posttest.explanation: Stack stores return addresses and local variables.

posttest.question: Which is NOT a valid ATmega328P port?
posttest.explanation: ATmega328P has only Port B, C, and D. No Port A.

posttest.question: What is a watchdog timer used for?
posttest.explanation: WDT auto-resets MCU when not periodically fed.

posttest.question: What does the SREG register contain?
posttest.explanation: SREG holds CPU flags and the global interrupt enable bit.

posttest.question: In an ISR, interrupts are automatically:
posttest.explanation: AVR clears I-flag in SREG upon entering an ISR.

feedback: Flawless emergency protocol! Watchdog timers are crucial to long-running, remote embedded devices where a human isn't present to push the reset button.
